retrieved_docs.title: Necrosis Pathology -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Irreversible cell injury and eventual cell death due to pathological processes are termed necrosis. It is an uncontrolled cell death that results in swelling of the cell organelles, plasma membrane rupture and eventual lysis of the cell, and spillage of intracellular contents into the surrounding tissue leading to tissue damage. [1] Unlike programmed cell death known as apoptosis which generates from intrinsic signals, necrosis occurs due to overwhelming noxious stimulus from outside the cell and is almost always associated with inflammatory responses due to the release of heat shock proteins, uric acid, ATP, DNA, and nuclear proteins, which cause inflammasome activation and secretion of proinflammatory cytokine interleukin-1 beta (IL1). [2] Typically, necrosis is not associated with caspase activation or normal development, but different types of regulated necrosis have been described, such as necroptosis, pyroptosis, and ferroptosis. like apoptosis, enzymatic pathways such as caspases, kinases, and the polyubiquitin system, have the main role in necroptosis and pyroptosis. [3] [4] [5] Necroptosis, especially, shares several key processes with both apoptosis and autophagy. [6] [7] Synchronized regulated necrosis is the result of specific lipid peroxidation in ferroptosis. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Trauma -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple complex pathways activate in response to damaged DNA, cellular stress, or infections. Here, we focus on the cellular response to mechanical trauma. Although there may be many mechanisms of traumatic injury to cells (physical damage or extreme temperature changes), the ultimate consequence is a loss of cellular integrity and function, eventually leading to cell death. Necroptosis is 1 mechanism in which cell death due to an injury can occur and is the most studied. The pathway begins with TNF alpha binding to its receptor, leading to the "RIPotosome" formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Apoptosis -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Current research suggests that apoptosis is one of the predominant cell death mechanisms, summarized below: Necroptosis: Occurs following activation of tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), triggering several cell death receptors. [5] Pyroptosis: Mainly affects cell membrane integrity, engaging inflammasomes to activate caspases. [5] [6] Apoptosis: Differentiated by the release of cytochrome c from  the  mitochondria, it is immunologically silent and non-lytic. [5] [6] Ferroptosis: Characterized by iron-dependent phospholipid peroxides that accumulate in cell membranes, leading to non-apoptotic death. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Trauma -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: After TNF binding to its corresponding receptor, TRAF2 (TNF receptor-associated factor 2) and TRADD (TNF-associated death domain protein) are signaled, which leads to the ligation of RIPK1 and RIPK3's recruitment. [2] The "RIPotosome" can also spontaneously form without TNF alpha binding in response to trauma. [3] Once RIPK3 gets phosphorylated by RIPK1, it causes oligomerization of MLKL. The exact mechanism of MLKL remains a mystery, but research proposes that MLKL disrupts the plasma membrane integrity via ion channels. The "RIPotosome" can be turned off via the ubiquitination of RIPK1. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Necrosis typically occurs when there is extensive damage to the cell membrane and internal structures that push the cell past reversible injury. It is usually the result of acute external factors, such as trauma, ischemia, toxins, or other non-physiologic mechanisms. Necrotic cell death results in a specific presentation such as damage to the plasma membrane, cellular swelling, and unregulated nuclear degradation.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Renal Tubular Necrosis -- Pathophysiology -- Later injury
retrieved_docs.content: These events are more pronounced in the corticomedullary junction of the kidney. In this phase, damage to the renal vascular endothelial cell is responsible for the ischemia of the renal tubular epithelial cell. The cells in the outer medulla continue to undergo injury and death with the combination of both necrosis and apoptosis. Apoptosis is the default mechanism to remove senescent cells and does not involve an inflammatory cascade. On the other hand, ferroptosis and necroptosis are the likely mechanisms underlying ATN, creating the characteristic "muddy brown casts" seen on urine microscopy. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Necrosis Pathology -- Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotic death is almost always associated with an inflammatory response. Necrotic cells release factors like high mobility group box 1 protein (HMGB1), and hepatoma-derived growth factor (HDGF). [18] [19] These factors are sensed by a nod-like receptor protein 3 (NLRP3), which is a core protein of the inflammasome. [20] This results in inflammasome activation and causes the release of the pro-inflammatory cytokine IL1β. NLRP3 inflammasome activation is triggered mainly through ATP produced by mitochondria released from damaged cells. [21] Necrosis does not typically correlate with activation of caspases, and it appears that it causes cell demise in response to damage or pathology, but not during normal development. Despite this, it turns out that a programmed form of necrotic death (termed necroptosis ) is very common in vivo, mainly in diverse forms of neurodegeneration and death inflicted by ischemia or infection. Unlike unordered necrosis, necroptosis is a more physiological and programmed type of necroptotic death and shares several key processes with apoptosis. It occurs due to the activation of the kinase domain of the receptor-interacting protein 1 (RIP1) and the assembly of the RIP1/RIP3-containing signaling complex. It is triggered by tumor necrosis factor (TNF) family members, needs caspase 8 inhibition, and assembly of necrosome (RIPK1-RIPK3 complex IIb). [9] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: If a cell is unable to adapt to increased stress, injury results. Cell injury is reversible until a certain threshold where it progresses to cell death. Historically, cell death has been designated into two classes: necrosis and apoptosis. Necrosis is often coined as accidental death as it is generally seen as not controlled by the cell. Apoptosis, on the other hand, is typically viewed as programmed cell death, regulated and controlled. The two differ in presentation, morphology, and mechanism though there has been recognized overlap, which will be discussed at the end of this review.

retrieved_docs.title: Necrosis Pathology -- Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: Morphologically, necrotic cells characteristically demonstrate by swelling of organelles, such as the endoplasmic reticulum and mitochondria, the rupture of the plasma membrane, and the lysis of the cell. [17] These changes cause cells to be more eosinophilic, glassy, and vacuolated. Loss of cell membrane integrity and compromise of organelle membranes adds to this. ATP depletion or decreased synthesis is the first biochemical change observed in injury. ATP is produced by oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria in the presence of oxygen. In necrosis associated with hypoxia or chemical injury, there is a lack of supply of oxygen to cells, leading to decreased ATP production. This lack of ATP results in the failure of the energy-dependent sodium pump in the plasma membrane, causing an influx of calcium and water, leading to cell swelling and detachment of ribosomes from the endoplasmic reticulum. Increased cytosolic calcium and oxidative stress lead to mitochondrial damage. Cytosolic calcium can also lead to the activation of several cytosolic enzymes, including phospholipases and proteases, which lead to the breakdown of both membranes(including lysosomal membranes) and proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: Apoptosis -- Anatomical Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: Subsequently, the condensed cytoplasm and nucleus break into fragments called apoptotic bodies that bud off from the cell. Macrophages then remove these apoptotic bodies in a process called efferocytosis. The cell membrane remains intact without inflammation, unlike necrosis, pyroptosis, or ferroptosis, where cell swelling and inflammation are common. Macrophages remove apoptotic cells quickly, with little or no inflammation occurring in the surrounding tissues. As such, the mechanism is considered immunologically silent. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Anthracycline Toxicity -- Etiology -- Mitochondrial-Mediated Cardiomyocyte Death
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines demonstrate a dose-dependent ability to alter the mitochondrial membrane permeability (MMP). This process is calcium- and redox-dependent, with mitochondrial iron playing a pivotal role. As MMP increases, the inability to maintain electrochemical gradients impairs mitochondrial function, promoting cellular respiration failure and necroptosis. [14] The increase in MMP also induces the release of apoptosis-inducing factor (AIF) and promotes cellular death through caspase-independent mechanisms separate from the cytochrome-C mechanism previously described; this is known as ferroptosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Apoptosis -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Given common mid-stream mediators, some researchers do not differentiate necroptosis from apoptosis as a separate mechanism. Instead, the simultaneous process is called PANoptosis, where pyroptosis, apoptosis, and necroptosis occur as programmed cell death. [9] In addition, autophagy refers to the process of digesting organelles or other parts of cells through lysosomal machinery, which can also lead to cell death. [10] Some parts of apoptosis are considered reversible, a phenomenon known as anastasis, particularly observed in cancer cell lines. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Free cytoplasmic calcium increases mitochondrial permeability by opening a transitional pore. Exposure of mitochondrial contents artificially releases cytochrome c (Cyt c), an important factor in the electron transport chain as well as in apoptosis and cell death. Cyt c begins a cascade of cell injury by direct activation of caspases. This would be one way that necrosis and apoptosis begin to overlap, as caspase activation is a primary mechanism of apoptotic death. In necrotic cell death, the release of caspases is coincidental to cellular injury. In apoptosis, caspase activation is purposeful and initiated through a series of regulating factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The cellular characteristics of necrotic cell death do not always follow the pattern seen in ischemic injury, but it serves as a good example. Necrosis results in cell lysis and exposure of cytoplasmic contents in an uncontrolled setting as the plasma membrane is damaged, regardless of the type of damage that led it to this stage. A release of cellular content into the surrounding area generally elicits an inflammatory response not seen in apoptosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell Death -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Ischemia, for example, typically ends in necrotic cell death. With the decreased delivery of oxygen to the cell, oxidative phosphorylation performed by the mitochondria becomes compromised. This results in a decreased amount of ATP available in the cytoplasm. In cells with a Na+/K+ (sodium-potassium) pump, lack of sufficient ATP disables the transporter. Sodium begins to increase within the cell as it can no longer be pumped out. This results in cellular swelling as the increase in intracellular sodium pulls water in. Cellular swelling, blebbing, and loss of microvilli are typical presentations of necrotic cell death. If ATP is restored in time, these presentations are reversible. If ATP is not restored before further cell damage, the loss of ATP progresses to have extensive effects on the cell.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Extrinsic Pathway of Apoptosis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Regardless of how an apoptotic pathway becomes initiated, there are also profound changes induced in the plasma membrane. Phosphatidylserine is normally restricted to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane. Upon initiation of apoptosis, phospholipid scramblase activity results in phosphatidylserine exposure on the outer leaflet of the cell, serving as a marker for engulfment by phagocytes. Mutations that interfere with the engulfment of apoptotic cells have been implicated in a variety of autoimmune diseases such as lupus (SLE). [13]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2562
retrieved_docs.content: Injury due to legal intervention by firearm and injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted by crashing of aircraft are both linked to the morphological finding of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The presence of تَلَف الأنسجة is itself associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that such traumatic events may involve neurotoxic processes alongside structural tissue injury. Recognizing the connection between these mechanisms can inform clinical assessment and management of patients with firearm‑related legal interventions or aircraft‑related trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2814
retrieved_docs.content: The allowed qualifier can be chemically induced in a range of medical conditions, including premature birth (ولادة قبل الأوان), tissue tear (تمزق), stone formation (حَصاة), and enteric viral infection (عدوى بفيروس معوي). In addition, hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) can be qualified by a chemically induced mechanism. This highlights that chemical induction serves as a common qualifying factor across diverse pathologies, linking developmental, traumatic, calculous, infectious, and metabolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” is connected to the pathophysiology of several distinct medical conditions, including diabetic kidney disease (اعتلال الكلية السكري), retinal detachment (انفصال الشبكية), threat of miscarriage (تهديد بالإجهاض), and fetal distress syndrome (متلازمة الضائقة الجنينية). Recognizing how the allowed qualifier relates to the underlying pathophysiological mechanisms of these disorders can help clinicians appreciate shared biological pathways and improve comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 867
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest typically results from a traumatic event and is characterized by tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة). This tissue damage may be accompanied by neurotoxic changes (سمية عصبية). An injury described as “injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of motor vehicle” is classified as a Motor vehicle traffic (MVT) injury and shares the same associated morphology of tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة). Both the general internal injury of chest and the motor‑vehicle‑related injury are linked through the common pathological finding of tissue damage, which can involve neurotoxicity, underscoring the need to evaluate the traumatic mechanism and potential toxic effects in patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 788
retrieved_docs.content: Injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of aircraft, and Injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of motor vehicle are both described as having the associated morphology of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة) is further characterized by the associated morphology of سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity). Moreover, the injury resulting from a motor vehicle crash is classified as Motor vehicle traffic (MVT). These connections indicate that injuries of uncertain intent arising from both aircraft and motor vehicle crashes share a common pathological feature of tissue damage that may involve neurotoxic mechanisms, and that the motor‑vehicle–related injury is specifically categorized within motor vehicle traffic incidents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2364
retrieved_docs.content: Asphyxiation and strangulation are medical events that are classified under the category of other injuries and conditions due to external causes, indicating that they arise from external mechanisms rather than internal disease processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity) through a has_associated_morphology relationship, suggesting that tissue damage in these injuries may involve neurotoxic processes. Overall, the network of relationships connects firearm‑related external cause codes, specific injury classifications, anatomical injury hierarchies, co‑occurring fracture patterns, and underlying pathological morphologies such as tissue damage and neurotoxicity, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding how diverse mechanisms of trauma converge on similar clinical and pathological outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 87
retrieved_docs.content: morphology for multiple injury types, including internal chest injury, **fracture of the cervical spine with cord lesion**, and **injuries of uncertain intent** caused by **aircraft crashes** or **motor‑vehicle crashes**; tissue damage is further linked to **neurotoxicity** (**سمية عصبية**). External cause codes for **firearm** classify both the specific injury due to **legal intervention by firearm** and the general e‑code category **firearm**, and the legal‑intervention injury also exhibits the tissue‑damage morphology. Injuries of uncertain intent from **motor‑vehicle crashes** are classified as **Motor vehicle traffic (MVT)**, which in turn defines the e‑code category **motor vehicle traffic (mvt)**. The e‑code for **cut/pierce** is related to a **cut wound** (**جرح قطع**), which is a subtype of an open wound (**جرح مفتوح**). Together, these relationships delineate how firearm‑related accidents, accidental physical contacts, and various cutting or piercing mechanisms interrelate

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 604
retrieved_docs.content: External cause codes: Firearm are classified as Injury due to legal intervention by firearm. Injury due to legal intervention by firearm is associated with the morphology of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). Fracture of cervical spine with cord lesion also has the associated morphology تَلَف الأنسجة, indicating that tissue damage is a common pathological feature of both firearm‑related legal intervention injuries and cervical spine fractures with spinal cord involvement. The tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة) is further linked to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), suggesting that the morphological consequence of tissue injury may involve neurotoxic processes. Recognizing these connections helps clinicians understand injury mechanisms, anticipate tissue and neural complications, and guide appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 833
retrieved_docs.content: generic unspecified abnormal‑finding categories, all of which fall within the larger umbrella of injuries and conditions arising from external causes, including toxic exposures such as heavy metals and physical mechanisms such as asphyxiation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2417
retrieved_docs.content: Infectious process is identified as a pathological mechanism underlying post‑abortion complications, and these complications are referred to in Arabic as “إجْهاضٌ مَصْحُوبٌ بعَدْوَى.” This relationship highlights that infection can be a key contributor to adverse outcomes following an abortion, emphasizing the importance of recognizing and managing infectious etiologies to prevent or treat post‑abortion complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: legal interventions, cervical spine fractures, cutting/piercing mechanisms, and neurotoxic tissue damage through shared classifications, hierarchical inverse‑isa and cause‑of relationships, and common morphological outcomes such as تَلَف الأنسجة, providing clinicians with an integrated framework for coding, diagnosing, and managing a wide spectrum of injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 87
retrieved_docs.content: a subtype of an open wound (**جرح مفتوح**). Together, these relationships delineate how firearm‑related accidents, accidental physical contacts, and various cutting or piercing mechanisms interrelate through injury types (open wound, puncture wound, penetrating wound), complications, tissue‑damage morphology, neurotoxicity, and standardized e‑code classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for documenting and managing trauma in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 239
retrieved_docs.content: classifications, co‑occurrence patterns, and shared pathological mechanisms such as tissue damage and neurotoxicity. Recognizing these interrelationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, coding, and management of patients who sustain complex traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2789
retrieved_docs.content: Chemicky indukované, which denotes a chemically induced etiology, is recognized as a qualifier that can be applied to several obstetric and neurological conditions. Specifically, the qualifier chemicky indukované may be used to describe خلل الحركة المتأخر (a late‑onset movement disorder), فطار غزوي (a premature rupture of membranes) and إجهاض تلقائي غير كامل (a spontaneous incomplete abortion). Moreover, chemicky indukované is explicitly allowed as a qualifier for إجهاض تلقائي غير كامل, indicating that a chemically induced mechanism is an accepted descriptor for this type of incomplete abortion. This qualification helps clinicians categorize the underlying cause of these conditions and informs appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 702
retrieved_docs.content: Bard‑Pic syndrome is linked to the morphological pattern of obstruction (انسداد), indicating that structural changes characteristic of this syndrome often involve obstructive processes. Chronic asthmatic bronchitis is also associated with obstruction, suggesting that airway inflammation in this condition can lead to or coexist with obstructive pathology. Obstructive renal disease (اعتلال الكلية الانسدادي) similarly shows an association with obstruction, reflecting the role of impaired urinary flow in renal impairment. The concept of obstruction (انسداد) serves as a central hub that connects to several related urological and renal entities: urinary tract obstruction (انسداد السبيل البولي), the condition referred to as خانُوق, yellow stagnation (رُّكودِ صفْراوِيّ), the lesion termed عِلَّوص, and kidney stone formation (موه الكلى). Moreover, obstruction is described as having a subordinate condition, infarct (انفتال), indicating that prolonged or severe blockage can result in tissue necrosis. Finally, obstruction maintains a direct relationship with bladder obstruction (انسداد المثانة), underscoring the cascade of downstream effects that an initial obstructive event can produce throughout the urinary system. Together, these interconnections highlight the pervasive impact of obstructive mechanisms across respiratory, renal, and urinary pathologies, emphasizing the importance of recognizing and managing obstruction to prevent secondary complications.
